segments.title: Reading treaty priorities from the delegates
segments.summary: The opening method treats each delegation as evidence of what its government needed from the Washington Naval Conference.
segments.body: The video begins by asking who each government chose to send, rather than assuming naval priorities were self-evident from the final treaty. Delegates brought political authority, imperial connections, diplomatic skill, or technical knowledge, and those choices reveal the interests they were expected to protect.

evidence.note: The speaker introduces the conference delegates and explains why their selection indicates national priorities.

segments.title: America’s priority: peace without a naval bill
segments.summary: Harding’s administration sought treaty limits that reduced a costly construction programme while keeping the United States outside wider political commitments.
segments.body: The discussion links Harding’s reluctance to join the League of Nations with a broader preference for limited federal spending and retained sovereignty. The proposed cancellation or non-completion of capital ships mattered because Congress had little appetite for sustaining wartime naval expenditure after 1918. The speaker also connects that fiscal mood to the difficulty of disposing of the enormous American merchant fleet acquired during the war.

evidence.note: Harding’s sovereignty concerns, debt policy, proposed ship reductions, and spending priorities are discussed.

segments.title: Why Washington sent Hughes, Lodge, Underwood, and Root
segments.summary: The American delegation combined executive influence, Senate leadership, bipartisan backing, and internationalist credibility to make an agreement ratifiable.
segments.body: Charles Evans Hughes led as secretary of state, while Henry Cabot Lodge and Oscar Underwood brought the leadership of both Senate parties into the delegation. Elihu Root added the authority of a former secretary of state and prominent peace advocate. The combination made the delegation an instrument for turning a negotiated settlement into a treaty that could pass the Senate.

evidence.note: The roles of Hughes, Lodge, Underwood, and Root are used to explain American political objectives.

segments.title: Britain’s postwar bargain between welfare and sea power
segments.summary: Lloyd George’s government wanted to lower military costs while preserving the imperial connections and naval position on which British security rested.
segments.body: The video presents postwar Britain as a coalition trying to fund domestic reconstruction while avoiding another expensive arms competition. Balfour, First Lord Arthur Lee, and Ambassador Auckland Geddes represented high political authority, naval administration, and a direct connection to Lloyd George. Their selection shows a government trying to reconcile economy with an empire whose trade and security still depended on maritime power.

evidence.note: Lloyd George’s defence spending views and the experience of the British delegates are explained.

segments.title: Canada’s low-cost route to Commonwealth security
segments.summary: Canada wanted the protection of a strong imperial system while concentrating its own resources on national development rather than large armed forces.
segments.body: Former prime minister Robert Borden represented a Canada shaped by the cost of the First World War and by Mackenzie King’s reluctance to fund defence. The speaker describes a preference for contributing finance to the British system instead of maintaining major Canadian forces. That preference encouraged support for treaty limits that kept the Royal Navy’s required burden manageable.

evidence.note: Borden, Mackenzie King, Canadian defence priorities, and the proposed dominion squadron scheme are discussed.

segments.title: Australia and New Zealand defend themselves in depth
segments.summary: The two Pacific dominions depended on naval power but differed sharply in their capacity to fund ships and crews of their own.
segments.body: George Pearce is presented as an Australian politician who understood sea power as essential to an island continent and had supported early Royal Australian Navy procurement. New Zealand, by contrast, had helped finance HMS New Zealand but lacked the personnel and resources to sustain a comparable force. Both dominions therefore had a direct interest in keeping the Royal Navy strong, because it supplied their wider defence in depth.

evidence.note: Pearce’s naval outlook and New Zealand’s limits on financing and crewing a cruiser force are described.

segments.title: India and South Africa weigh naval investment differently
segments.summary: South Africa saw little need to spend heavily at sea, while India’s representative linked future nation-building to the possibility of an indigenous navy.
segments.body: South Africa delegated representation to Balfour and is described as prioritising land forces and infrastructure over naval expenditure. India’s delegate, V. S. Srinivasa Sastri, is presented as an unusually persuasive diplomatic voice whose long-term concern was building Indian political and maritime capacity. The discussion uses hypothetical cruiser allocations to show how treaty choices could affect the development of imperial naval forces.

evidence.note: South African spending priorities and Sastri’s role in representing India are discussed.

segments.title: France cannot cover both colonies and the Mediterranean
segments.summary: Postwar demographic and economic damage left France unable to finance every naval mission it faced, especially colonial security and parity with Italy.
segments.body: The video frames French policy through the human and industrial damage of the First World War, alongside unstable coalition politics. Its delegates’ colonial backgrounds are read as evidence that overseas possessions were a central concern. Yet matching Italy in the Mediterranean consumed forces that France also needed for a dispersed empire, creating a strategic dependence on British naval strength.

evidence.note: France’s postwar recovery problems, colonial concerns, and Mediterranean balance with Italy are explained.

segments.title: Italy’s simple objective: naval equality with France
segments.summary: Italian policy focused on equality with France, a treaty outcome that could confine French power to either Mediterranean defence or colonial commitments.
segments.body: The speaker argues that Italian planners identified parity with France as the one diplomatic objective that mattered most in the Mediterranean. Italy’s shipyards and earlier competition with Austria are used to challenge a simple picture of Italian naval weakness. Equality did not merely decide a paper ranking: it limited France’s ability to cover both its home waters and overseas empire at the same time.

evidence.note: Italian parity with France, shipyard standardisation, and Italian delegates are discussed.

segments.title: Japan’s delegation and the power outside the signatures
segments.summary: Japan’s team combined naval and diplomatic officials with Prince Tokugawa, whose informal standing mattered even without a formal signature.
segments.body: The video places the negotiations amid the assassination of Hara Takashi and the uncertain relationship between civilian politics, the navy, and the military. Admiral Tomosaburo Katō headed the formal delegation, but Prince Tokugawa Iesato supplied influence rooted in the upper house and wider Japanese political life. British and Italian delegates recognised that informal authority, while Americans initially focused too narrowly on the official list of signatories.

evidence.note: Japanese political circumstances, Katō’s delegation, and Prince Tokugawa’s informal influence are discussed.

segments.title: The 5:5:3 ratio: economy for some, insecurity for Japan
segments.summary: The treaty’s capital-ship ratio reduced spending, but the video argues that its lower Japanese allocation also deepened fears that future conflict was becoming more likely.
segments.body: The conclusion separates the American desire for cheaper peace from the security consequences of the agreed ratios. A 5:5:3 allocation looked modest to Britain and the United States, but it fell short of the 7:10 relationship that Japanese leaders considered safer. The discussion then explores how slightly higher permitted totals might have preserved industrial capacity and produced very different capital-ship and carrier fleets.

evidence.note: The American result, Japanese concern over the 5:5:3 ratio, and alternative treaty limits are discussed.
